title: SLI, SLO, Error Budgets, and Burn Rate
description:
## Define the Error Budget

Your reliability docs state:

- **SLI:** the proportion of HTTP requests served successfully in under 300 ms.
- **SLO:** 99.9% of such requests over a rolling 28-day window.

During sprint planning, a new engineer asks what the team's *error budget* is and how it relates to the burn-rate alert that paged the on-call yesterday.

Which statement is correct?

options.text: The error budget is `1 − SLO` over the window — here 0.1% (about 40 minutes of unreliability over 28 days). Burn rate is how fast the budget is being consumed relative to that allowance; a high (fast) burn rate over a short window means the budget will be exhausted quickly, which is why it pages.
The error budget is the SLO itself (99.9%): the budget of good requests you are allowed to serve. Burn rate measures how quickly you are using up those successful requests.
The error budget equals the SLI minus the SLO, so it only exists when current performance is below target; if the SLI is above 99.9% there is no error budget and burn-rate alerts cannot fire.
The error budget is a fixed count of allowed incidents per quarter agreed with management; burn rate is the number of incidents opened divided by the quarter length.

explanation: The error budget is the allowable amount of unreliability over the SLO window: `error budget = 1 − SLO`. With a 99.9% SLO, that's 0.1% of requests (or, expressed as time, roughly 40 minutes over 28 days) that may fail the SLI before the SLO is breached. Burn rate normalizes consumption: a burn rate of 1 means you'd exactly exhaust the budget over the full window; a burn rate of, say, 14.4 means you'd burn the entire 28-day budget in ~2 days. Fast-burn alerts (high burn rate measured over a short window) page because they indicate the budget is being destroyed rapidly; slow-burn alerts use longer windows and lower urgency. (b) confuses the budget with the SLO — the budget is the failure allowance (1 − SLO), not the success target. (c) is wrong: the budget is a fixed allowance defined by the SLO over the window regardless of current performance; you consume it as failures occur, and it does not vanish when you're healthy (in fact a healthy SLI means you're consuming little of it). (d) is wrong: the error budget is derived quantitatively from the SLO and SLI, not an arbitrary negotiated incident count.

hints.title: Budget = allowed failure
hints.body: If the SLO is the target success rate, the budget is what's left for failure: 1 minus the SLO over the window.

hints.title: Why fast burn pages
hints.body: Burn rate is consumption speed relative to the budget. Burning it fast over a short window means imminent SLO breach, hence a page.
